subject: Chemistry
chapter: Haloalkanes and Haloarenes; Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers

questions.question: Which reagent is used in the Swarts reaction to convert an alkyl chloride into the corresponding alkyl fluoride?
questions.explanation: The Swarts reaction uses metallic fluorides such as AgF, Hg2F2, CoF2 or SbF3 to prepare alkyl fluorides from alkyl chlorides or bromides.

questions.question: Which product is formed when bromoethane is treated with aqueous KOH?
questions.explanation: Aqueous KOH favours nucleophilic substitution, replacing bromine with a hydroxyl group to form ethanol.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing reactivity towards SN1 reaction: Ethyl chloride, tert-Butyl chloride, Isopropyl chloride.
options: Ethyl chloride > Isopropyl chloride > tert-Butyl chloride
tert-Butyl chloride > Ethyl chloride > Isopropyl chloride
Isopropyl chloride > tert-Butyl chloride > Ethyl chloride
tert-Butyl chloride > Isopropyl chloride > Ethyl chloride
Ethyl chloride > tert-Butyl chloride > Isopropyl chloride
Isopropyl chloride > Ethyl chloride > tert-Butyl chloride
questions.explanation: SN1 reactivity follows carbocation stability: tertiary > secondary > primary.

questions.question: Which reagent is required for the Finkelstein reaction?
questions.explanation: The Finkelstein reaction converts alkyl chlorides or bromides into alkyl iodides using sodium iodide in dry acetone.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing ease of nucleophilic substitution: Ethyl chloride, Chlorobenzene, tert-Butyl chloride.
questions.explanation: Haloarenes are least reactive due to resonance and partial double-bond character of the C–Cl bond. Among alkyl halides, tertiary compounds react readily by SN1.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why chlorobenzene is less reactive than chloroethane towards nucleophilic substitution?
options: Chlorobenzene has a longer C–Cl bond.
The C–Cl bond in chlorobenzene has partial double-bond character due to resonance.
The chlorine atom is positively charged.
The benzene ring behaves as a nucleophile.
questions.explanation: Resonance gives the C–Cl bond in chlorobenzene partial double-bond character, making bond cleavage difficult.

questions.question: Which product is obtained when ethyl chloride is treated with alcoholic KOH?
questions.explanation: Alcoholic KOH favours β-elimination, producing ethene.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing boiling point: Chloroethane, Ethane, Ethanol.
questions.explanation: Alcohols exhibit hydrogen bonding, haloalkanes have dipole-dipole interactions, while alkanes possess only weak van der Waals forces.

questions.question: The major product formed when methylmagnesium bromide reacts with carbon dioxide followed by acidic hydrolysis is:
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents react with carbon dioxide to give carboxylic acids containing one additional carbon atom after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Which nucleophile produces an isocyanide when reacted with an alkyl halide?
questions.explanation: AgCN is largely covalent and attacks through nitrogen to produce isocyanides, whereas KCN attacks through carbon to produce nitriles.

questions.question: Which reagent converts a primary alcohol into an alkyl chloride while producing only gaseous by-products?
questions.explanation: Thionyl chloride forms alkyl chloride along with SO2 and HCl gases, giving a relatively pure product.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing boiling point: Dimethyl ether, Ethanol, Propane.
questions.explanation: Alcohols exhibit hydrogen bonding, ethers show dipole-dipole interactions and alkanes possess only dispersion forces.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing solubility in water: Ethanol, Propan-1-ol, Butan-1-ol.
questions.explanation: Water solubility decreases with increase in the length of the alkyl chain.

questions.question: Which alcohol does NOT undergo oxidation under ordinary conditions with acidified KMnO₄ or K₂Cr₂O₇?
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols lack an α-hydrogen and therefore resist oxidation under ordinary conditions.

questions.question: Phenol reacts with bromine water to give:
questions.explanation: Phenol rapidly reacts with bromine water to give a white precipitate of 2,4,6-tribromophenol.

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing reactivity towards Lucas reagent: Ethanol, tert-Butyl alcohol, Propan-2-ol.
questions.explanation: Lucas reagent reacts fastest with tertiary alcohols, followed by secondary and then primary alcohols.

questions.question: Which named reaction converts sodium phenoxide into salicylic acid after treatment with carbon dioxide and acidification?
questions.explanation: The Kolbe reaction introduces a carboxyl group into sodium phenoxide to form salicylic acid.

questions.question: Which reagent is used in the Reimer-Tiemann reaction?
questions.explanation: Phenol reacts with chloroform and aqueous sodium hydroxide in the Reimer-Tiemann reaction to give salicylaldehyde.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing acidity: Ethanol, Water, Phenol.
questions.explanation: Phenol is most acidic due to resonance stabilization of the phenoxide ion, whereas ethanol is the least acidic.

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains why phenol is more acidic than ethanol?
options: Phenol has a lower molecular mass.
Phenol contains an aromatic ring only.
questions.explanation: The conjugate base of phenol is resonance stabilized, making proton loss easier than in ethanol.

questions.question: Which compound is obtained by oxidation of propan-2-ol using acidified potassium dichromate?
questions.explanation: Secondary alcohols are oxidized to ketones. Propan-2-ol gives propanone (acetone).

questions.question: Arrange the following in decreasing boiling point: Propan-2-ol, Propan-1-ol, 2-Methylpropan-2-ol.
questions.explanation: Boiling point decreases with branching because branching reduces the surface area available for intermolecular attractions.

questions.question: Which reagent is commonly used to oxidise a primary alcohol to an aldehyde without further oxidation?
questions.explanation: Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC) selectively oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes without significant oxidation to carboxylic acids.

questions.question: Which compound is formed when ethanol is oxidised with PCC?
questions.explanation: PCC oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes. Hence ethanol gives ethanal.

questions.question: Which reagent converts an acid chloride into an aldehyde by partial reduction?
questions.explanation: The Rosenmund reduction converts acid chlorides into aldehydes using hydrogen over poisoned palladium supported on barium sulfate.

questions.question: The Rosenmund reduction is used to prepare:
questions.explanation: Rosenmund reduction is a selective method for preparing aldehydes from acid chlorides.

questions.question: Which reagent converts a nitrile into an aldehyde followed by hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: DIBAL-H partially reduces nitriles to imines, which on hydrolysis produce aldehydes.

questions.question: Which reagent is used in the Rosenmund reduction for the preparation of aldehydes from acid chlorides?
questions.explanation: In the Rosenmund reduction, acid chlorides are reduced to aldehydes using hydrogen gas over poisoned palladium supported on barium sulfate.

questions.question: Which product is obtained when propan-1-ol is oxidised using acidified potassium dichromate under controlled conditions?
questions.explanation: Under controlled oxidation, a primary alcohol first forms the corresponding aldehyde.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing ease of oxidation: Propan-2-ol, 2-Methylpropan-2-ol, Propan-1-ol.
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation, secondary alcohols oxidise to ketones and primary alcohols oxidise most readily.

questions.question: Which alcohol produces acetone on oxidation?
questions.explanation: Oxidation of propan-2-ol produces propanone (acetone).

questions.question: Which reagent is commonly used to prepare ketones from secondary alcohols?
questions.explanation: Secondary alcohols are oxidised to ketones using oxidising agents such as acidified potassium dichromate or potassium permanganate.

questions.question: Which reagent converts an acid chloride into a ketone?
questions.explanation: Acid chlorides react with dialkylcadmium reagents to form ketones without further addition.

questions.question: Which product is obtained when ethanoyl chloride reacts with dimethylcadmium?
questions.explanation: Dimethylcadmium reacts with ethanoyl chloride to produce propanone (acetone).

questions.question: Which compound is produced when benzonitrile is partially reduced with DIBAL-H followed by hydrolysis?
questions.explanation: DIBAL-H selectively reduces nitriles to aldehydes after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing ease of oxidation: Ethanol, Propan-2-ol, 2-Methylpropan-2-ol.
questions.explanation: Tertiary alcohols resist oxidation, secondary alcohols oxidise less readily than primary alcohols.

questions.question: Which reagent is used in the Stephen reaction for the preparation of aldehydes from nitriles?
questions.explanation: The Stephen reaction involves reduction of nitriles using stannous chloride and hydrochloric acid followed by hydrolysis to form aldehydes.

questions.question: The Stephen reaction converts which functional group into an aldehyde?
questions.explanation: Stephen reduction converts nitriles into aldehydes after hydrolysis.

questions.question: Which product is obtained when propan-2-ol is oxidised using acidified potassium permanganate?
questions.explanation: Secondary alcohols are oxidised to ketones. Hence propan-2-ol gives propanone.

questions.question: Which reagent is NOT suitable for preparing aldehydes from primary alcohols without significant over-oxidation?
questions.explanation: Acidified KMnO₄ is a strong oxidising agent and generally oxidises aldehydes further to carboxylic acids.

questions.question: Which compound is obtained when propan-1-ol undergoes mild oxidation with PCC?
questions.explanation: PCC selectively oxidises primary alcohols to aldehydes.

questions.question: Arrange the following in increasing ease of oxidation: Methanol, Propan-2-ol, tert-Butyl alcohol.
questions.explanation: Primary alcohols oxidise most readily, followed by secondary alcohols, whereas tertiary alcohols resist oxidation.

questions.question: Which reagent is used to prepare ketones by hydration of alkynes in the presence of mercuric sulphate?
questions.explanation: Hydration of alkynes using dilute sulphuric acid and mercuric sulphate produces an enol, which undergoes tautomerisation to form a ketone.

questions.question: Which compound is formed when propyne undergoes hydration in the presence of HgSO₄ and dilute H₂SO₄?
questions.explanation: Propyne first forms an enol which rapidly tautomerises to propanone.

questions.question: The immediate product formed after hydration of an alkyne, before ketone formation, is:
questions.explanation: Hydration of alkynes initially gives an enol, which undergoes keto-enol tautomerism to form the more stable carbonyl compound.

questions.question: Which process converts an enol into its corresponding aldehyde or ketone?
questions.explanation: Keto-enol tautomerisation converts the unstable enol into the more stable aldehyde or ketone.

questions.question: Which reagent is used for the hydration of alkynes to prepare ketones according to the NCERT method?
questions.explanation: NCERT describes hydration of alkynes using dilute sulphuric acid in the presence of mercuric sulphate catalyst.

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains why ketones are formed after hydration of alkynes?
options: The enol formed is more stable than the ketone.
The initially formed enol rapidly undergoes keto-enol tautomerisation.
Mercuric sulphate directly converts alkynes into ketones without any intermediate.
Water acts as an oxidising agent.
The reaction proceeds through an SN1 mechanism.
The alkyne first forms a carboxylic acid.
questions.explanation: Hydration produces an enol intermediate, which spontaneously rearranges to the more stable keto form.

questions.question: Which compound gives propanone after hydration followed by tautomerisation?
questions.explanation: Hydration of propyne in the presence of HgSO₄/H₂SO₄ gives an enol that tautomerises to propanone.

questions.question: Which statement regarding keto-enol tautomerisation is correct?
options: The keto form is generally more stable than the enol form.
The enol form is always the major product at equilibrium.
Tautomerisation occurs only in the presence of oxygen.
Ketones cannot exist in equilibrium with enols.
questions.explanation: For most simple carbonyl compounds, the keto form is thermodynamically more stable than the enol form, so equilibrium lies predominantly towards the keto form.
